37.play: Than this for whom we render'd up this woe.

2.play: [Enter LEONATO, ANTONIO, BENEDICK, BEATRICE, MARGARET, URSULA, FRIAR FRANCIS, and HERO]

3.play: FRIAR FRANCIS: Did I not tell you she was innocent?

4.play: LEONATO: So are the prince and Claudio, who accused her

5.play: Upon the error that you heard debated:

6.play: But Margaret was in some fault for this,

7.play: Although against her will, as it appears

8.play: In the true course of all the question.

9.play: ANTONIO: Well, I am glad that all things sort so well.

10.play: BENEDICK: And so am I, being else by faith enforced

11.play: To call young Claudio to a reckoning for it.

12.play: LEONATO: Well, daughter, and you gentle-women all,

13.play: Withdraw into a chamber by yourselves,

14.play: And when I send for you, come hither mask'd.

16.play: The prince and Claudio promised by this hour

17.play: To visit me. You know your office, brother:

18.play: You must be father to your brother's daughter

19.play: And give her to young Claudio.

20.play: ANTONIO: Which I will do with confirm'd countenance.

21.play: BENEDICK: Friar, I must entreat your pains, I think.

22.play: FRIAR FRANCIS: To do what, signior?

23.play: BENEDICK: To bind me, or undo me; one of them.

24.play: Signior Leonato, truth it is, good signior,

25.play: Your niece regards me with an eye of favour.

26.play: LEONATO: That eye my daughter lent her: 'tis most true.

27.play: BENEDICK: And I do with an eye of love requite her.

28.play: LEONATO: The sight whereof I think you had from me,

29.play: From Claudio and the prince: but what's your will?

30.play: BENEDICK: Your answer, sir, is enigmatical:

31.play: But, for my will, my will is your good will

32.play: May stand with ours, this day to be conjoin'd

33.play: In the state of honourable marriage:

34.play: In which, good friar, I shall desire your help.

35.play: LEONATO: My heart is with your liking.

37.play: Here comes the prince and Claudio.

38.play: [Enter DON PEDRO and CLAUDIO, and two or three others]

39.play: DON PEDRO: Good morrow to this fair assembly.

40.play: LEONATO: Good morrow, prince; good morrow, Claudio:

41.play: We here attend you. Are you yet determined

42.play: To-day to marry with my brother's daughter?

43.play: CLAUDIO: I'll hold my mind, were she an Ethiope.

44.play: LEONATO: Call her forth, brother; here's the friar ready.

46.play: DON PEDRO: Good morrow, Benedick. Why, what's the matter,

47.play: That you have such a February face,

48.play: So full of frost, of storm and cloudiness?

49.play: CLAUDIO: I think he thinks upon the savage bull.

50.play: Tush, fear not, man; we'll tip thy horns with gold

51.play: And all Europa shall rejoice at thee,

52.play: As once Europa did at lusty Jove,

53.play: When he would play the noble beast in love.

54.play: BENEDICK: Bull Jove, sir, had an amiable low;

55.play: And some such strange bull leap'd your father's cow,

56.play: And got a calf in that same noble feat

57.play: Much like to you, for you have just his bleat.

58.play: CLAUDIO: For this I owe you: here comes other reckonings.

60.play: Which is the lady I must seize upon?

61.play: ANTONIO: This same is she, and I do give you her.

62.play: CLAUDIO: Why, then she's mine. Sweet, let me see your face.

63.play: LEONATO: No, that you shall not, till you take her hand

64.play: Before this friar and swear to marry her.

65.play: CLAUDIO: Give me your hand: before this holy friar,

66.play: I am your husband, if you like of me.

67.play: HERO: And when I lived, I was your other wife:

69.play: And when you loved, you were my other husband.

72.play: One Hero died defiled, but I do live,

73.play: And surely as I live, I am a maid.

74.play: DON PEDRO: The former Hero! Hero that is dead!

75.play: LEONATO: She died, my lord, but whiles her slander lived.

76.play: FRIAR FRANCIS: All this amazement can I qualify:

77.play: When after that the holy rites are ended,

78.play: I'll tell you largely of fair Hero's death:

80.play: And to the chapel let us presently.

81.play: BENEDICK: Soft and fair, friar. Which is Beatrice?

83.play: BENEDICK: Do not you love me?

84.play: BEATRICE: Why, no; no more than reason.

85.play: BENEDICK: Why, then your uncle and the prince and Claudio

86.play: Have been deceived; they swore you did.

87.play: BEATRICE: Do not you love me?

88.play: BENEDICK: Troth, no; no more than reason.

89.play: BEATRICE: Why, then my cousin Margaret and Ursula

90.play: Are much deceived; for they did swear you did.

91.play: BENEDICK: They swore that you were almost sick for me.

92.play: BEATRICE: They swore that you were well-nigh dead for me.

93.play: BENEDICK: 'Tis no such matter. Then you do not love me?

94.play: BEATRICE: No, truly, but in friendly recompense.

95.play: LEONATO: Come, cousin, I am sure you love the gentleman.

96.play: CLAUDIO: And I'll be sworn upon't that he loves her;

97.play: For here's a paper written in his hand,

98.play: A halting sonnet of his own pure brain,

101.play: Writ in my cousin's hand, stolen from her pocket,

103.play: BENEDICK: A miracle! here's our own hands against our hearts.

104.play: Come, I will have thee; but, by this light, I take

106.play: BEATRICE: I would not deny you; but, by this good day, I yield

107.play: upon great persuasion; and partly to save your life,

108.play: for I was told you were in a consumption.

109.play: BENEDICK: Peace! I will stop your mouth.

111.play: DON PEDRO: How dost thou, Benedick, the married man?

112.play: BENEDICK: I'll tell thee what, prince; a college of

113.play: wit-crackers cannot flout me out of my humour. Dost

114.play: thou think I  care for a satire or an epigram? No:

115.play: if a man will be beaten with brains, a' shall wear

116.play: nothing handsome about him. In brief, since I do

117.play: purpose to marry, I will think nothing to any

118.play: purpose that the world can say against it; and

119.play: therefore never flout at me for what I have said

120.play: against it; for man is a giddy thing, and this is my

121.play: conclusion. For thy part, Claudio, I did think to

122.play: have beaten thee, but in that thou art like to be my

123.play: kinsman, live unbruised and love my cousin.

124.play: CLAUDIO: I had well hoped thou wouldst have denied Beatrice,

125.play: that I might have cudgelled thee out of thy single

126.play: life, to make thee a double-dealer; which, out of

127.play: question, thou wilt be, if my cousin do not look

129.play: BENEDICK: Come, come, we are friends: let's have a dance ere

130.play: we are married, that we may lighten our own hearts

133.play: BENEDICK: First, of my word; therefore play, music. Prince,

134.play: thou art sad; get thee a wife, get thee a wife:

135.play: there is no staff more reverend than one tipped with horn.

137.play: Messenger: My lord, your brother John is ta'en in flight,

138.play: And brought with armed men back to Messina.

139.play: BENEDICK: Think not on him till to-morrow:

140.play: I'll devise thee brave punishments for him.
